coredata.dc:title: S,N-Doped carbon dots for tetracyclines sensing with a fluorometric spectral response
coredata.dc:creator: Xing, Xuejian
Huang, Li
Zhao, Shaojing
Xiao, Jiafu
Lan, Minhuan
coredata.dc:description: S,N-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) were facilely prepared via a hydrothermal method by using thiourea and citric acid (CA) as precursors. The as-prepared S,N-CDs exhibited high stability in aqueous solution and were applied for fluorometric detection of tetracyclines (TCs). The approach was developed on the basic of the fluorescence of S,N-CDs at 440 nm, which was selectively quenched by TCs through the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Moreover, the fluorescence of TCs with maximum at 600 nm could be used as the internal standard signal, resulting in a fluorometric spectral response. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.25, 0.39 and 0.26 μM for tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DC) and oxytetracycline (OTC), respectively. Furthermore, S,N-CDs were successfully utilized for the detection of residual TCs in fish and pork samples with acceptable recovery and good RSD.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Doping
Fluorometric probe
Tetracyclines

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of S,N-CDs
Absorption and fluorescence spectral assay of TCs and the limit of detection (LOD) of TCs
Detection of TCs in fish and pork samples
The effect of solvent on TCs sensing
Detection of TC in fish and pork samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) TEM image of S,N-CDs (inset: HR-TEM image of one S,N-CD). b) The particle size distribution histograms (n = 25) of S,N-CDs. c) AFM image of S,N-CDs (inset: height of S,N-CDs on a mica substrate). d) Raman spectrum of S,N-CDs.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) XPS spectrum and the high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s and (d) N1s of S,N-CDs.

a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of S,N-CDs. The inset shows the photograph and fluorescent images of S,N-CDs under 365 nm UV light irradiation. b) Fluorescence spectra of S,N-CDs with the excitation wavelength from 300 to 640 nm. Comparison of (c) photostability and (d) photobleaching characteristics between S,N-CDs and fluorescein sodium under xenon lamp (10 mW/cm2) irradiation.
Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity ratio (I600/I440) of S,N-CDs in the absence and presence of 50 μM TC.

a) The change in fluorescence spectra of S,N-CDs with gradually increasing concentrations of TC in 10 mM PBS buffer at pH 7.4. b) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (I600/I440) and different concentrations of TC. The inset shows the corresponding fluorescence change with and without TC. c) The change in fluorescence intensity ratio (I600/I440) with addition of 50 μM TC in 5 min. d) The fluorescence and excitation spectra of S,N-CDs, and the absorption spectra of TC.

Selectivity of S,N-CDs for TC with added (a) Antibiotics, including (2) amoxicillin, (3) penicillin, (4) carbenicillin, (5) ampicillin, (6) chloramphenicol, (7) streptomycin, (8) gentamicin, (9) kanamycin, and (10) erythromycin, (b) Metal ions, including (2) Na+, (3) K+, (4) Ba2+, (5) Ca2+, (6) Mn2+, (7) Fe2+, (8) Fe3+, and (9) Al3+, and (c) Anions, including (2) S2−, (3) F−, (4) CO3 2−, (5) PO4 3−, (6) Cl−, (7) SO4 2−, and (8) NO3 −. (1) in (a–c) represents the pure S,N-CDs solution with or without added 50 μM TC.
Preparation of S,N-CDs and ratiometric fluorescent probe for TCs detection.
Determination and spiked recoveries for fish and pork samples.

head.ce:title: S,N-Doped carbon dots for tetracyclines sensing with a fluorometric spectral response

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: S,N-doped carbon dots (S,N-CDs) were facilely prepared via a hydrothermal method by using thiourea and citric acid (CA) as precursors. The as-prepared S,N-CDs exhibited high stability in aqueous solution and were applied for fluorometric detection of tetracyclines (TCs). The approach was developed on the basic of the fluorescence of S,N-CDs at 440 nm, which was selectively quenched by TCs through the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Moreover, the fluorescence of TCs with maximum at 600 nm could be used as the internal standard signal, resulting in a fluorometric spectral response. The limit of detection (LOD) was 0.25, 0.39 and 0.26 μM for tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DC) and oxytetracycline (OTC), respectively. Furthermore, S,N-CDs were successfully utilized for the detection of residual TCs in fish and pork samples with acceptable recovery and good RSD.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracyclines (TCs), including tetracycline (TC), doxycycline (DC) and oxytetracycline (OTC), possess a broad spectrum of anti-bacterial activity and are widely used in pharmaceuticals and food additives [1]. However, the residual TCs in environment and food pose a serious threat to human health [2,3]. Various methods have been established to sensitively and selectively detect TCs, such as capillary liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [4], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [5], and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6]. Although those techniques have some advantages, they require sophisticated and expensive instrumentation, complicated pretreatment steps and long analysis times, which limit their applications in daily inspections. Therefore, development of new approach toward sensitive and selective detection of antibiotics is a significant research issue from both scientific and technical aspects.

Recently developed sensing methods such as electrochemical, colorimetric and fluorescent methods are of practical importance for the detection of chemicals in various areas such as food, environment and medicine [7–12]. Among these methods, fluorescence technique featured with rapid, convenient, low cost, high sensitivity and good reliability for TCs detection [13–17]. Various types of fluorescent materials have been developed to detect TCs, which include organic dyes [18], conjugated polymers [19], metallic nanoparticles [20], silicon quantum dots (QDs) [21], semiconductor QDs [22], and carbon dots (CDs) [23]. The doped CDs and other carbon nanostructures can be prepared conveniently by simple hydrothermal or pyrolysis routines and are widely used in the fields of optics, environment and energy duo to their unique structure and excellent property [24–32]. Interestingly, doped CDs as novel carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial have unique optical properties, facile surface functionalization, outstanding water solubility, which were especially suitable for bioimaging and biosensing applications [33–36]. For instance, Li and coauthors prepared blue fluorescent S,N-CDs using glutathione as precursor, the fluorescence was quenched by TC through inner filter effect and the LOD was 0.04 μM [37]. Huang and co-workers synthesized N-CDs by hydrothermal treatment of rice residue with ethylglycine at 200 °C, which exhibited highly sensitive detection of TC with LOD of 0.24 μM [38]. Zhang et al. obtained S,N-CDs through a hydrothermal route using CA and ammonium thiocyanate as raw materials, and the resulting fluorescence CDs could specifically recognize DC with LOD of 0.02 μM [39].

However, the above-mentioned CDs-based fluorescence probes utilize fluorescence intensity quenching as the single signal output, which was easily influenced by environmental conditions, concentration of probe and instrumental efficiency, thus limiting their practical applications. In contrast, fluorometric probes can effectively address the abovementioned disadvantages by measuring the fluorescence intensity ratios of two or more emission peaks with different wavelengths [40–42]. Liu et al. constructed Eu3+ modified CDs (Eu3+-CDs) as ratiometric fluorescent probe for TCs detection. They found that the blue emission of CDs at 442 nm remained almost unchanged, whereas the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 616 nm significantly increased in the presence of TC [43]. Zhou et al. designed and prepared SiO2 coated red and green CDs as fluorometric probes for the selective detection of TC with recording the red to blue emission ratio [44]. However, these CDs-based fluorescent probes need to introduce other fluorophores.

Here, S,N-CDs as fluorescent sensor was developed for TCs detection with ratiometric fluorescent spectral response. As shown in Scheme 1 , S,N-CDs were conveniently synthesized using thiourea and CA as precursor. The as-prepared S,N-CDs showed blue fluorescence (λmax = 440 nm under 340 nm excitation). The fluorescence of S,N-CDs was sensitively and selectively quenched by TCs through IFE mechanism. Moreover, the orange fluorescence of TCs with peak at 600 nm could be used as the internal standard signal to rectify the instrumental efficiency and environmental interferences [45,46]. Owing to above features, S,N-CDs were applied to establish a ratiometric fluorescent system for detecting TCs, including TC, DC and OTC with a broad detection range from 0.5 to 140 μM. More importantly, the S,N-CDs were further successfully applied for detecting residual TC in fish and pork samples with acceptable recoveries ranging from 100.4% to 113.8% and good RSD.

CA and thiourea were provided by Heomns Chemistry Reagent Company. Amoxicillin, penicillin, carbenicillin, ampicillin, chloramphenicol, streptomycin, gentamicin, kanamycin, erythromycin, TC, DC, and OTC were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company. NaF, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, BaCl2, MnCl2, FeSO4, FeCl3, Al2(SO4)3, Na2S, K3PO4, Na2CO3, Na2SO4, KNO3 were obtained from Energy Chemistry Reagent Company. All the purity of reagents was in analytical level and directly used as received.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using TEM (Tecnai G2 F20) and AFM (Nano-Scope Bruker) in the tapping mode, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were collected by a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer using KBr pellets. UV–vis absorption spectra and the fluorescence spectra were obtained on a Shimadzu UV-2600 and Shimadzu Rf-6000, respectively.

The S,N-CDs were synthesized by referring to previously reported method [47]. Briefly, 0.42 g (2 mmol) CA, 0.46 g (6 mmol) thiourea and 10 mL deionized water were added into beaker, and ultrasonicated until completely dissolved to obtain a homogeneous and transparent solution and then that was transferred into autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the S,N-CDs were collected via filtration with a 0.22 μm membrane and dialysis against deionized water. The purified S,N-CDs were stored at 4 °C for characterization and use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of S,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 was chosen as the reference. The FLQY of S,N-CDs aqueous solution was calculated based on the following equation:
φ S
=
φ R
×
I S
/
I R
×
A R
/
A S
×
η S
/
η R
2
where φ is the FLQY, Ι is the area of the integrated fluorescence spectrum, η is the solvent refraction index, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength, the subscript “R” and “S” are reference and S,N-CDs, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and fluorescence spectral assay of TCs and the limit of detection (LOD) of TCs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical method, 15 μL S,N-CDs (0.2 mg/mL) solution and different concentration of TCs standard solution were added into 2 mL PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4). After incubating at room temperature for 30 s, the fluorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. All fluorescence spectra were performed in triplicate to ensure statistic purpose.

The LOD of TCs was calculated according to following equation:
LOD =
3 δ
s
where δ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity ratio (I600/I440) of S,N-CDs (n = 20), s is the slope of linear relationship about the fluorescence intensity ratio (I600/I440) of S,N-CDs versus the concentration of TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in fish and pork samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fish and pork were purchased from local market and treated according to reported literature [48]. Briefly, 2.0 g skinless fish or pork was smashed, and then immersed in 5 mL acetonitrile/deionized water (V/V = 7/3) mixture. The obtained sample was firstly homogenized by vortex for 3 min and ultrasonicated for 15 min, and then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particle. The supernatant was filtrated by using 0.22 μm filter membrane, and diluted to 100 mL by adding PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4).

The morphology and crystalline structure of S,N-CDs were studied by TEM AFM. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, S,N-CDs showed good dispersion and nearly spherical shape with the different sizes ranging from 2.7 to 4.8 nm. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 1a clearly revealed the crystalline structure with a lattice space of 0.21 nm, which is according with the [1 0 0] lattice distance of graphene along the [0 0 1] direction [49]. The AFM image shown in Fig. 1c revealed a topographic height of ∼3.5 nm, which is in good agreement with the TEM observation. Raman image of S,N-CDs showed two characteristic peaks at 1325 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1, which corresponded to the disorder (D band) and graphite (G band) nanostructure of the CDs, respectively (Fig. 1d). It is known that the ratio of I D/I G is related to the extent of graphitization. The I D/I G of S,N-CDs was 0.59, demonstrating these S,N-CDs were highly graphitic [50].

FT-IR spectrum of S,N-CDs was observed in Fig. 2 a, the peaks of NH and OH stretching vibration were in region of 3000–3600 cm−1. The characteristic peaks at 1743, 1583 and 1401 cm−1 confirmed the presence of CO CC and C-N, respectively. C-O bond at 1124 cm−1, C-S bond at 1204 cm−1 were also observed. Furthermore, XPS was carried out to further analyze the S,N-CDs surface elemental composition. The S,N-CDs displayed five peaks located at 532, 401, 285, 230 and 165 eV, which corresponded to O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s and S2p, respectively (Fig. 2b). The atomic ratio of C/N/O/S was 42/9/45/4. The high-resolution C1s spectrum displayed four peaks, i.e. CC at 284.4 eV, C-N/C-O/C-S at 285.3 eV, CO at 286.9 eV and COOH at 288.9 eV (Fig. 2c). The presence of graphitic N at 400.1 eV, pyrrolic N at 401.2 eV, and amino N at 401.8 eV were determined from the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 2d). Combining the above results with the FT-IR and XPS results, we confirmed that the surface of S,N-CDs was abundant in OH, NH2 and COOH groups.

As exhibited in Fig. 3 a, three absorption peaks were observed at 234, 342 and 595 nm, in which the two peaks at 234 and 342 nm may be assigned to the π → π* transition of the CC bond and n → π* transition of the CO bond, respectively [51]. The peak at 595 nm may be related to the π → π*, n → π* transition of the CS or SO bond [47]. The S,N-CDs aqueous solution is blue and transparent under sunlight, while it exhibited blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation (inset of Fig. 3a). The FLQY was measured to be 2.3% by using quinine sulfate as the standard. Moreover, S,N-CDs exhibited an excitation wavelength-dependent feature in region of 300–480 nm (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence emission peaks were gradually red-shifted from 420 to 580 nm upon increasing excitation wavelength, and the fluorescence intensity first increased and then decreased. The maximum fluorescence intensity with an emission peak at 440 nm was obtained under 340 nm light excitation.

The photostability of S,N-CDs was evaluated using fluorescein sodium as reference. As shown in Fig. 3c and d, under xenon lamp (10 mW/cm2) irradiation, the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of fluorescein sodium gradually decreased over time. In contrast, the absorbance and fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs almost unchanged, indicating their excellent photostability (Fig. S1). Moreover, the fluorescence spectrum of S,N-CDs collected in 0–10 mM NaCl and showed no obvious variation (Fig. S2). This result reveals the excellent chemical stability of S,N-CDs, which would be beneficial for their biosensing and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of solvent on TCs sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH of PBS on the sensing performance of S,N-CDs was further studied. As revealed in Fig. S3a, the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs was increased as the pH increased from 4 to 9, and no wavelength shift was recorded. The fluorescence emission of TCs was blue-shifted with increase in the pH from 4 to 9 (Fig. S3b–d), inducing the fluorescence peak of TCs is close with that of S,N-CDs so that interfere with each other. To largely eliminate this disadvantage, a pH of 7.4 was chose. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I600/I440) remained at ∼0.09 in the pH range of 4 to 9. However, after addition of 50 μM TC, the I600/I440 ratio increased significantly to over 0.6 (Fig. 4 ). Interestingly, TC sensing was also carried out in other solvent (Fig. S4). PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4) was chose as solvent duo to its safety and non-interference in biomedical study.

The performance of S,N-CDs for sensing TCs was studied in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) by collecting fluorescence spectra of S,N-CDs with different concentrations of TCs. Fig. 5 a displayed the fluorescence of S,N-CDs located at 440 nm was quenched significantly by adding TC, while a fluorescence emission band at around 600 nm attributed to TC was appeared. The fluorescence intensity ratio of I600/I440 showed a linear increase upon increasing of TC from 0 to 60 μM, demonstrating that S,N-CDs can act as fluorescent sensor to construct ratiometric fluorescent system for sensing TC (Fig. 5b). The LOD was calculated to be 0.25 μM. With further addition of TCs above 60 μM, the fluorescence intensity of TCs decreased slightly, which may be result from the aggregation-induced quenching effect of TCs (Fig. S5a–c). Kinetic study revealed that S,N-CDs responded rapidly to TC with a reaction time of ∼30 s (Fig. 5c). The TC-induced fluorescence quenching of S,N-CDs may be ascribed to the inner filter effect [45]. As shown in Fig. 5d, the excitation spectrum of S,N-CDs matched well with the absorption spectrum of TC. S,N-CDs exhibited similar fluorescence spectral response to other TCs, such as DC and OTC, with the corresponding limits of detection being 0.39 and 0.25 μM, respectively (Figs. S6 and S7).

TC, DC and OTC leaded to similar change of fluorescence spectra because of their similar chemical composition and structure. Therefore, TC was chose as instance to determine the selectivity of S,N-CDs towards TCs. The interference effect of other antibiotics, metal ions and anions on the fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs was studied. As shown in Fig. 6 a–c (red columns), the I600/I440 ratio of S,N-CDs appeared only slight change in the presence of 50 μM of the foreign species. Only the addition of TC with 50 μM significantly increased the I600/I440 ratio (black columns). These results demonstrated the outstanding selectivity of S,N-CDs for TCs detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC in fish and pork samples
ce:section.ce:para: TCs are widely used as antibiotics in animal breeding industries to prevent and treat infections. Hence, the detection of TCs residue in meat is very important for food safety. Different amounts of TC were added to pretreated fish and pork samples to evaluate the feasibility of S,N-CDs for practical application. As shown in Table 1 , upon addition of 10, 25, 50 and 100 μM of TC, the recoveries of TC ranged from 100.4% to 113.8%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was within 1.7%–9.0%. This result demonstrates that S,N-CDs-based ratiometric fluorescent strategy for TC detection with good accuracy and precision.

In summary, S,N-CDs were prepared from CA and thiourea by facile hydrothermal treatment and then applied as fluorescent senor for ratiometric detection of TCs. The prepared S,N-CDs exhibited an extensive absorption band from UV region to visible region, and showed excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission behaviors ranging from 300 nm to 480 nm. Upon the increasing of TCs, the blue emission at 440 nm was significantly decreased due to the inner filter effect mechanism, and the fluorescence of TCs located at 600 nm was intensified. A good linear relationship existed between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I600/I440) and the concentration of TC was observed. The limits of detection for TC, DC and OTC were calculated to be ∼0.25, 0.39 and 0.26 μM, respectively. More importantly, S,N-CDs were further successfully applied for detecting residual TC in fish and pork samples with acceptable recoveries varying from 100.4% to 113.8% and good RSD.

S,N-CDs prepared from hydrothermal treatment of thiourea and citric acid were developed to sensitively and selectively detect TCs with fluorometric spectral response. The application of S,N-CDs in sensing the residual TC in fish and pork samples were also successfully achieved with satisfactory recovery and excellent RSD.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (No. 2018JJ3632 and 2019JJ50824), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of Central South Universities (No. 2019zzts126 and 2019zzts849), and Basic Research Foundation of Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation (No. JCYJ20180307151245919).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
